title: extract_api_key accepts unsanitized Bearer tokens (CRLF/control char injection surface)
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

Dimension: Security and Access

Location: src/proxy.rs lines 1198-1211 (fn extract_api_key).

Violation: The function prefers `Authorization: Bearer ...` header but falls back to `x-api-key`. Two issues:
  (a) It accepts whatever string appears after 'Bearer ' — no validation. A header value like `Bearer \r\nHost: evil.com` passed through unchanged becomes a header-injection vector downstream when we then interpolate into `format!("Bearer {api_key}")` at line 1390 / 1413. reqwest's HeaderValue layer will likely catch this, but the proxy code never checks.
  (b) The 'or_else' branch at line 1204 also uses v.to_str() with no sanitization; an x-api-key header with embedded control chars (e.g., 0x00, 0x0A) will silently become the api_key String that is then interpolated into a Bearer header for a non-Anthropic provider.
  (c) No rate-limiting or length cap — an 8KB api_key header passes through as a Rust String allocation.

Mandated Refactor:
  1. Introduce `fn extract_api_key(headers: &HeaderMap) -> Option<ApiKey>` where ApiKey is the redacting newtype from issue #256.
  2. ApiKey::from_header_value validates: must be ASCII printable, no \r\n, length < 512. Return None (or Err at the perimeter) on violation.
  3. When Authorization header exists but lacks 'Bearer ' prefix, log WARN and refuse (currently silently returns None).
  4. Add a test that injects `Authorization: Bearer test\r\nX-Injected: 1` and asserts the request returns 400 before reaching the provider.
